why: Joseph MacRory was a Catholic cardinal and Archbishop of Armagh.
q: What is the religion of Joseph MacRory?
why2: Joseph MacRory (1861-1945) was an Irish Roman Catholic cardinal and Archbishop of Armagh; 'Catholic' is correct and responsive.

why: The most prominent Museum of Islamic Art is in Doha, Qatar.
q: In what country is Museum of Islamic Art?
why2: Ambiguous referent: both the Doha museum (Qatar) and the Cairo museum (Egypt) are literally named 'Museum of Islamic Art'; the answer fits only the Doha reading.

why2: 'Symphony No. 3' is written by many composers (Beethoven, Brahms, Mahler, Saint-Saens, Copland, Gorecki...); Beethoven fits only one of many equally plausible readings.

why: Patty Jenkins directed Monster (2003), the most plausible intended film.

why2: Multiple notable films are titled exactly 'Monster' (2003 Patty Jenkins, 2021 Anthony Mandler, 2023 Hirokazu Kore-eda); the answer fits only the 2003 reading.

why2: Ninja Boy II (Game Boy, 1993, Culture Brain) is an action game with RPG elements (action role-playing); 'Action' is a factually correct, responsive genre for it.

why2: Multiple notable Joseph Schuberts exist; 'Catholic' fits the Romanian Roman Catholic bishop Joseph Schubert (1890-1969), but the question does not determine which Joseph Schubert (e.g. the 18th-century Dresden composer) is meant.

why: Alexander the Great's father was Philip II of Macedon.
q: Who is the father of Alexander?
why2: Bare 'Alexander' under-determines the referent; 'Philip II of Macedon' is correct only for the Alexander-the-Great reading, not for the many other Alexanders (e.g. Russian tsars) the question could denote.

why: Bran Castle is marketed as 'Dracula's Castle'.
q: Bran Castle that is located on the border between Transylvania and Wallachia in Romania is marketed as the home of which sinister figure?
why2: Bran Castle is famously marketed as 'Dracula's Castle', the home of the sinister figure Dracula.

why: The phrase is Spock's Vulcan salute from Star Trek, so the stated association is factually
q: Who is associated with the words ‘Live long and prosper’?
why2: The question asks WHO; the canonical answer is Mr. Spock (a Vulcan character) — 'Star Trek' names the franchise, not a person, so the answer is non-responsive to the question as written.

why: Lenny Bruce was a stand-up comedian, i.e. worked in comedy.
q: Lenny Bruce was in what field of entertainment in the 1960s?
why2: Lenny Bruce was a stand-up comedian/satirist, so 'Comedy' is the correct field of entertainment.

why: Turnbull & Asser is the famous shirtmaker located on Jermyn Street, London.

why: Apollo 11 launched July 16, 1969 and achieved the first crewed moon landing.

why2: Apollo 11 launched July 16, 1969 and was the first mission to land humans on the Moon.

q: What is the name of the basket used to keep bees?
why2: The question asks for the name of the basket, which is a 'skep'; 'beehive' is the generic term for any bee dwelling, not the name of the basket.

why: The Masters has been played at Augusta National since 1934.
q: The U.S. Masters, one of the four major championships in professional golf, is the only major championship that is played at the same course each year. Where has it been played since its inception in 1934?
why2: The Masters has been played at Augusta National Golf Club (Augusta, Georgia) since 1934.

why: The femur is the standard quiz answer for the strongest/hardest bone.
q: What is the Human body's hardest bone?
why2: The femur is the longest/strongest bone; the hardest bone is the petrous part of the temporal bone (often quizzed as the jawbone), not the femur.

why: His mother was Stanley Ann Dunham, known as Ann.
q: What was the first name of the mother of US President Barack Obama?
why2: Her legal first name was Stanley (Stanley Ann Dunham); 'Ann' was her middle name, though she was universally known as Ann — correct under the 'name she went by' reading, wrong under the strict 'first name' reading.

q: At which battle of 2 December 1805 did Napoleon defeat a combined Austrian-Russian army?
why2: The Battle of Austerlitz, 2 December 1805, was Napoleon's victory over the combined Austro-Russian army.

why2: Tumbrils (tumbrels) were the open carts used to carry the condemned to the guillotine.
